title: How to build proactive agents & self-improving company (Fully explained)
summary: The video explains how to build proactive AI agents that improve themselves through feedback loops. It covers foundational concepts of autonomous AI systems, use cases in ads optimization and SEO, and tools like memory and agent frameworks that enable self-improving behaviors.
writeup: The video explains how to build self-improving AI loops for business operations, distinguishing closed-loop autonomous agents (which capture feedback and update their own strategy) from the more common AI-enhanced workflow (which completes tasks but has no feedback path back to the system). It walks through the architecture concretely: a memory layer split into factual logs and procedural skills, cron-scheduled execution, and data-access skills for structured inputs agents cannot otherwise reach. The through-line is that this architecture is already producing measurable results at real companies, not a theoretical proposal.
key_points: YC's current batch reports 5x revenue per employee versus 18 months ago, with agents autonomously writing 45 of their own tools and running internal operations end-to-end, establishing that closed-loop AI operations are no longer experimental.

The critical gap between 'AI-enhanced workflow' and 'AI-native loop' is feedback: in an enhanced workflow a human still drives prioritization and triggering, while a true AI loop captures outcomes, extracts learnings, and updates its own strategy without human intervention each cycle.

A functional AI loop requires five elements: a data ingestion layer, a policy layer encoding SOPs, system-access layers (APIs and CLIs), quality gates for output evaluation, and a mechanism to fold learnings back into the agent's skills.

Memory must be split into two types: factual memory (temporal logs of what the agent did and what outcomes resulted) and procedural memory (distilled learnings encoded as reusable skills), because unstructured logs become unretriefvable at scale.

Giorgio's autonomous ads experiment ran for one month with a cron-and-skill loop: the agent tested 10 ad formats in week one, identified that low-production 'ugly' whiteboard assets outperformed polished ones, and converged on that format in week two, generating 243 leads on a $1,500 budget.

LoopMe (open-source, built by the presenter's team) is a memory layer optimized for long-horizon self-iterating tasks: it scaffolds artifact types and cron jobs through a conversational setup, then runs daily crons that extract learnings and propose skill updates automatically.
